summary: Gruber highlights ICEBlock, a crowdsourced iOS app that lets users anonymously report ICE activity in their area, modeled after Waze. He notes the app is exclusively available on iOS because Apple's push notification system allows true anonymity without storing device IDs, unlike Android's requirements. Gruber draws a pointed parallel to Apple's 2019 removal of HKmap.live from the App Store under pressure from China, suggesting the Trump administration may similarly demand ICEBlock's removal. He argues that unlike HKmap.live, ICEBlock has no web fallback — it can only exist as a native iOS app — making Apple's decision about whether to defend it far more consequential.
key_points: ICEBlock is an iOS-exclusive app that crowdsources anonymous ICE sighting reports with no stored user data, automatic deletion after four hours, and a 5-mile radius alert system
The app cannot exist on Android or as a web app because only iOS push notifications allow delivery without storing device IDs or creating user accounts
Apple faced a similar situation in 2019 when it removed HKmap.live under Chinese government pressure, but that app had a functional website as fallback — ICEBlock has no such alternative
The stored device ID data that Android would require creates vulnerability to law enforcement subpoenas and hackers, making the platform choice a civil liberties issue
Gruber implicitly challenges Apple to stand by its privacy principles domestically after failing to do so with China
money_quotes: ICEBlock is interesting in and of itself, as will be Apple's response if (when?) the Trump administration takes offense to the app's existence.
The app broke no Hong Kong laws, but scared the thin-skinned skittish lickspittles in the Chinese Communist Party.
Removing the app from the App Store thus did not prevent Hongkongers from accessing it.
To maintain anonymity and store zero user data, there is and can be no web app version of ICEBlock. There is and can be no Android version.
Only iOS supports the security and privacy features for ICEBlock to offer what it does, the way it does.
Here's to hoping that Apple will proudly defend it if push comes to shove.
What 'brown shirts' were to the Nazis, we should make 'red hats' to MAGA.
key_insight: ICEBlock's iOS exclusivity — driven by Apple's superior privacy architecture for push notifications — means that if the Trump administration pressures Apple to remove it, there is no fallback, making Apple's response a definitive test of whether it will defend privacy principles at home as readily as it capitulated abroad.
